content.text: Every token an LLM samples is drawn from a probability distribution computed from raw logit scores. Two parameters —

— let you reshape that distribution before sampling occurs, reducing the chance the model echoes tokens it has already produced. Understanding how they differ, when to apply them, and when to keep them at zero is essential for anyone building reliable agentic systems.

Frequency and presence penalties are real-valued scalars that modify the raw logit scores assigned to each vocabulary token before the softmax step. They were introduced in the OpenAI API and have since been adopted (sometimes under different names) by many inference providers and local runtimes.
The two parameters serve overlapping but distinct purposes.
penalises tokens in proportion to how many times they have already appeared in the generated text: the more a token was used, the heavier its logit is pushed down.
applies a fixed, one-time penalty to any token that appeared at least once, regardless of how many times. Both are applied simultaneously, and their effects are additive.
In the OpenAI API the accepted range for both parameters is
. Positive values discourage repetition; negative values actively encourage it. Zero (the default) leaves logits unmodified.

In short, single-turn conversations agentic pipelines generate far more tokens — reasoning traces, tool call schemas, retry explanations, accumulated observations. Two failure modes arise from that scale:

An agent stuck on a tool error may reproduce the same apology paragraph or the same malformed JSON structure across dozens of turns. This wastes context window space and masks the actual error from downstream code.

Long-context completions sometimes drift into repeating transitional phrases ('Additionally…', 'It is worth noting…') or restating conclusions already reached. This degrades the quality of summaries and reports produced by agent steps.

Mild penalties can interrupt both patterns without measurably harming output quality for prose tasks. However, the same parameters can silently corrupt structured outputs — a misconfigured penalty will steer the model away from legitimate repeated tokens like
in JSON bodies, producing schema violations that are hard to trace.
Getting the defaults right (both at zero for tool-calling agents; mild positive values for open-ended prose agents) is therefore a first-line defence against two distinct failure families.
A small presence penalty (0.2–0.5) is often enough to interrupt stutter without any prompt changes, saving engineering time.
Frequency penalty pushes the model toward synonyms and varied phrasing, improving readability of long prose outputs.
Stopping a repeating agent trace early by making repeated phrases less likely means the context window is used for genuinely new information.
The parameters are applied at inference time via a single API field, requiring no training, no prompt engineering, and no post-processing.
Penalties work alongside temperature, top-p, and top-k rather than replacing them, letting practitioners tune multiple axes independently.
JSON tool schemas, YAML configs, and code all require predictable repeated tokens. Penalties depress their logits and cause malformed output.
Above ~0.8, frequency penalty forces the model to choose unusual words simply to avoid repetition, producing stilted text.
Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, and local runtimes (llama.cpp, Ollama) use different parameter names, ranges, and algorithms. Code is not portable.
At temperature < 0.3 the model already tends toward its top token; penalties on top can force truly improbable tokens to the surface.
Not a substitute for reasoning fixes.
Penalties treat the symptom (repeated tokens) not the cause (poor state management, missing stop signals). Over-reliance hides agent design flaws.
At each decoding step the model's final hidden state is projected onto the vocabulary to produce a vector of raw logit scores
. Before applying softmax, the runtime subtracts a penalty term from the logit of every token that appears in the already-generated sequence.
is the number of times token
appeared in the prompt plus generated text so far, and
is 1 if the token has appeared at all (else 0).
Softmax is then applied to the adjusted logits to produce a probability distribution from which the next token is sampled:

A key subtlety: the penalties scale with the value of the logit gap. If a token's raw logit is already much higher than competitors, a small penalty may not meaningfully change the sampling outcome. The effective impact depends on the model's confidence in that token.
Think of frequency penalty as a progressive tax: the first occurrence costs nothing, the second occurrence costs
, and so on. The model is eventually priced out of a heavily used token. Presence penalty is a flat toll: the model pays
the moment a token has been seen, no matter how many additional times.

For breaking early stutter, presence penalty responds faster because it takes effect after a single occurrence. Frequency penalty is gentler and lets commonly needed tokens appear once or twice before the pressure builds.

The diagram below shows where penalties slot into a single decode step. The model's raw logit vector is read from both the frequency and presence penalty modules (which maintain a token count map over the context), combined into adjusted logits, passed through softmax, and sampled.

attrs.alt: Frequency and Presence Penalty logit adjustment pipeline

content.text: Use moderate presence penalty for open-ended prose agents

When an agent generates conversational explanations, summaries, or planning narratives, a presence penalty in the 0.2–0.5 range prevents early phrase lock-in. Set it at the agent framework level so every step in a trace benefits from the same baseline.
Use mild frequency penalty for creative or long-form content

Writing tasks benefit from vocabulary diversity. Frequency penalty of 0.3–0.6 gently encourages the model to explore synonyms while still allowing common transition words to appear when genuinely needed.
Use zero penalties for structured output
Tool calls, JSON schemas, SQL queries, and YAML configurations depend on exact repeated tokens. Any non-zero penalty risks producing malformed output because the model learns to avoid
, or closing braces after they have appeared once.
Agents that must echo input fields back in their output (e.g., extraction tasks that mirror entity names)
Code generation — variable names and keywords need repetition
Any workflow with grammar-constrained decoding or JSON mode enabled
Low-temperature setups already near-deterministic — penalties amplify instability

Implementation is a single API parameter addition. The main decision points are: which agent steps need penalties, what values to set, and how to monitor for regressions in structured output validity.
Identify which agent steps produce prose output vs. structured output.
Apply non-zero penalties only to prose steps.
Start with presence_penalty=0.3, frequency_penalty=0.0 and evaluate.
Increase gradually in 0.1 increments while measuring output quality.
Add a structured-output validation check that alerts if JSON parse rate drops.
Log the raw penalty values alongside outputs for later analysis.

import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])


def agent_step_prose(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Agent step for narrative / reasoning output — penalties enabled."""
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.7,
        presence_penalty=0.3,   # one-time nudge against any already-seen token
        frequency_penalty=0.1,  # progressive pressure for heavily repeated tokens
        max_tokens=800,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content


def agent_step_structured(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Agent step that must return JSON — penalties must be zero."""
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.2,
        presence_penalty=0.0,   # MUST be 0 for JSON output
        frequency_penalty=0.0,  # MUST be 0 for JSON output
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content
Example 1: comparing penalty effect on synonym diversity

import os, json
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

prompt = [{"role": "user", "content":
    "List 10 adjectives meaning 'fast'. Return a JSON array of strings only."}]

configs = [
    {"name": "no penalties",        "presence_penalty": 0.0, "frequency_penalty": 0.0},
    {"name": "light presence",      "presence_penalty": 0.4, "frequency_penalty": 0.0},
    {"name": "light frequency",     "presence_penalty": 0.0, "frequency_penalty": 0.4},
    {"name": "both moderate",       "presence_penalty": 0.4, "frequency_penalty": 0.3},
]

for cfg in configs:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=prompt,
        temperature=0.8,
        presence_penalty=cfg["presence_penalty"],
        frequency_penalty=cfg["frequency_penalty"],
        max_tokens=100,
    )
    words = json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
    unique = len(set(w.lower() for w in words))
    print(f"{cfg['name']:25s}  unique={unique}/10  words={words}")

This script sends the same prompt four times with different penalty combinations. With no penalties a model at high temperature already produces diverse synonyms — the output from this experiment serves as a baseline to verify whether penalties are actually needed for a given task.
Example 2: detecting and fixing a repetition loop in an agent

import os, re
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])


def count_phrase_repeats(text: str, min_len: int = 6) -> dict[str, int]:
    """Return phrases of min_len+ words that appear more than once."""
    words = text.lower().split()
    counts: dict[str, int] = {}
    for size in (min_len, min_len + 2, min_len + 4):
        for i in range(len(words) - size + 1):
            phrase = " ".join(words[i:i + size])
            counts[phrase] = counts.get(phrase, 0) + 1
    return {ph: c for ph, c in counts.items() if c > 1}


def run_agent_step(messages, presence_penalty=0.0, frequency_penalty=0.0):
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.7,
        presence_penalty=presence_penalty,
        frequency_penalty=frequency_penalty,
        max_tokens=600,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content


# Simulate a long agent trace that tends to repeat
long_context = [
    {"role": "user", "content":
        "Summarise the history of computing from 1940 to 2000 in 400 words. "
        "Be thorough and mention key milestones."},
]

# Step 1: run without penalties, inspect for repetition
output_no_penalty = run_agent_step(long_context)
repeats = count_phrase_repeats(output_no_penalty)
print("Repeated phrases (no penalty):", list(repeats.keys())[:3])

# Step 2: if repetition detected, rerun with light penalties
if repeats:
    output_with_penalty = run_agent_step(
        long_context, presence_penalty=0.35, frequency_penalty=0.15
    )
    repeats_after = count_phrase_repeats(output_with_penalty)
    print("Repeated phrases (with penalty):", list(repeats_after.keys())[:3])

A production multi-agent document analysis pipeline processes legal contracts in three stages: extraction (structured JSON), analysis (prose reasoning), and report generation (narrative). Each stage calls the same model but with different penalty configurations.

The team originally used identical settings for all three stages. After enabling presence_penalty=0.4 globally, JSON parse errors increased from 0.2% to 4.1% — a 20× spike. Separating the config by stage reduced parse errors back below 0.3% while still eliminating the repetition loops seen in prose stages.

Using a single API wrapper that adds penalties to every call without checking the step type is the most common mistake. Tool-call steps break silently because JSON parse failures may not surface until downstream code reads the response.
Using high values to compensate for bad prompts

Setting penalties above 0.8 to silence a stubborn loop is a symptom of a deeper problem — typically a missing stop condition, an overly long context encouraging the model to recap, or a system prompt that incentivises restating goals on each turn. Fix the prompt first.
Assuming all providers honour the same range
OpenAI uses [-2.0, +2.0]. Azure OpenAI mirrors this. Ollama and llama.cpp use
with a default of 1.1 (multiplicative, not additive). Passing an OpenAI-calibrated value of 0.4 to llama.cpp has no effect because the parameter is named differently.
Add them only when you have observed a concrete repetition problem, not as a precaution.
Segment your agent by output type.
Keep a config object or dataclass per agent step type so penalty settings travel with the step definition.
Measure output quality and structured-output validity at each step before committing to a value.
Never exceed 0.6 for frequency_penalty in agentic contexts.
Beyond this point vocabulary distortion typically outweighs the repetition reduction benefit.
Log penalty values with each completion.
When debugging unexpected outputs, knowing the active penalty value narrows the search space immediately.
Use presence_penalty rather than frequency_penalty as your primary lever.
Presence penalty is gentler and easier to reason about — it fires once per token type rather than compounding.
Add a JSON validity check to your CI/integration tests.
Any pipeline that produces structured output should assert that parse rates remain above a threshold when configuration changes.

Penalty computation is O(|V|) per decode step — the runtime must scan the token history to build the count map, then apply adjustments across the full vocabulary. In practice this is negligible compared to the transformer forward pass, which dominates latency by several orders of magnitude.

However, the count map must be maintained across the full context, including the prompt. For very long contexts (100k+ tokens) the history scan grows, though inference providers implement this efficiently using prefix trees or simple hash maps. Callers do not need to manage this.

From a throughput perspective, penalties have no effect on batch size or KV-cache reuse. They are applied post-logit and do not alter the model weights or attention pattern, so all standard scaling strategies (batching, speculative decoding, KV caching) remain fully compatible.

) actively boost the probability of repeated tokens. This is rarely useful in practice but can be leveraged for tasks requiring constrained repetition, such as generating structured repeating patterns or performing token-level red-teaming evaluations.
The canonical interface. Parameters are passed directly in

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain recursion in three paragraphs."}],
    presence_penalty=0.3,
    frequency_penalty=0.1,
)
, pass parameters in the constructor or override per step via

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Constructor-level defaults
llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    temperature=0.7,
    model_kwargs={
        "presence_penalty": 0.3,
        "frequency_penalty": 0.1,
    },
)

# Per-call override (e.g. for a structured step inside a graph)
structured_llm = llm.bind(
    presence_penalty=0.0,
    frequency_penalty=0.0,
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
(window size for counting repeats). These are not equivalent to the OpenAI additive parameters and require independent calibration.

# Ollama CLI — repeat_penalty > 1.0 discourages repetition
ollama run llama3.1 --option repeat_penalty 1.2 --option repeat_last_n 64 \
  "Summarise the history of machine learning."
System prompt instructions to vary language
Penalties operate purely on output probability and cannot introduce data leakage or prompt injection on their own. However, two reliability concerns deserve attention in production:

A non-zero penalty on a structured output step produces syntactically incorrect JSON or YAML. The model does not throw an error — it simply generates the most probable token excluding over-penalised ones. The failure surface is subtle: a missing closing brace, an omitted required field, or a corrupted key name. Add explicit schema validation after every structured step and alert on validation failures.

Penalties make the output non-deterministic in a way that is correlated with prior tokens rather than the seed alone. Two runs with the same seed and temperature=0 will produce identical output, but if the context differs by even one token the penalty map changes and output diverges. Teams relying on deterministic replay for audit trails must include the full context — not just the seed — in their replay specification.

If your application exposes generation parameters to end users, validate that penalty values remain in a safe range. Negative penalties can cause runaway repetition loops that inflate token usage and response latency, functioning as a cheap denial-of-service vector against per-token cost budget controls.
subtracts a penalty proportional to how many times a token has appeared;
applies a fixed penalty the moment a token has been seen at all — the two are additive and complementary.
zero for any step that must produce structured output
(JSON, YAML, SQL, code). Even small values can corrupt schema keys and cause silent parse failures.
For open-ended prose generation, start with
, then tune in 0.1 increments while measuring output quality.
— they reshape the sampling distribution at inference time with negligible latency overhead and full compatibility with batching and KV caching.
Parameter names and semantics differ by provider: OpenAI uses additive scalars on [-2, +2]; llama.cpp/Ollama use a multiplicative
typically near 1.1. Code is not portable without adjustment.
last resort, not a first resort
. If an agent loops, fix the stop condition or system prompt first. Persistent repetition that requires penalties above 0.6 signals a deeper architectural problem.

Add a structured-output validity assertion to integration tests. Any configuration change — including penalty adjustments — should trigger an automated check that JSON parse rates remain above threshold.
